title: Hash ring implementation; foundational partitioning primitive used conceptually across many other designs

Idempotency and transaction patterns; highest correctness bar in the collection
Algorithm implementations (token bucket, sliding window); cross-cutting API protection concern
Order matching engine; reveals low-latency data structure choices (order book, price-time priority)
Real-time messaging architecture; different flavor from request/response systems
Stream processing and windowed aggregation; event-driven architecture patterns

Inventory concurrency control; pessimistic vs optimistic locking patterns
File sync and conflict resolution; potentially CRDT or OT patterns
Distributed crawl frontier; URL scheduling, politeness, deduplication

`get_stats` measures theoretical ring arc ownership while `get_distribution` measures empirical key counts; understanding when these diverge matters for capacity planning
The clockwise walk for replication is where virtual-node-aware deduplication happens; worth tracing with a small ring to understand edge cases (e.g., fewer physical nodes than requested replicas)
Google's "consistent hashing with bounded loads" adds a capacity cap per node to prevent hotspots even when the ring is balanced; a natural next step for this implementation
See how quorum failures, conflict resolution, anti-entropy, and hinted handoff are exercised in tests
The fallback to brute-force comparison means this doesn't get the O(log n) diffing benefit of a real Merkle tree; worth understanding the gap
The backup-node write in `put` is a sloppy quorum (write goes to a node not in the preference list); trace how this interacts with `deliver_hints` to restore strict quorum membership
`VectorClock.prune` discards low-counter entries, which can cause false concurrency detection; worth reasoning through when this matters
How the delivery semantics (at-least-once, at-most-once, exactly-once), rebalancing, and DLQ are tested; reveals edge cases the implementation handles
How the stock exchange reuses this message queue as an event bus for order matching, showing cross-module integration
Kafka's range vs. round-robin vs. sticky assignors compared to the simple modular assignment used here

The retry/backoff strategy and how it interacts with the injectable processor

Compare with the digital wallet implementation which likely tackles similar double-entry and balance concerns from a different angle
See how each algorithm is exercised, especially edge cases around window boundaries and token refill timing

How the matching engine is tested, especially edge cases like partial fills, self-trade prevention, and multi-level sweeps
The original system design plan, which likely covers scalability considerations (sharding by symbol, sequencer architecture) that this single-threaded implementation doesn't address
The matching algorithm is the heart of the system; trace through a multi-level sweep scenario (market buy consuming multiple ask levels) to understand the nested loop structure
Real exchanges sometimes use pro-rata allocation or size-priority at certain price levels; understanding why FIFO is the default and when alternatives apply
See what scenarios the tests cover: edge cases in pagination, offline delivery, group membership transitions
Understand the design rationale and what tradeoffs were considered before implementation
The cursor-based pagination logic has subtle edge cases around forward vs backward traversal and cursor position resolution
How this single-server Lamport clock approach would change with multiple servers (vector clocks, hybrid logical clocks, or server-assigned timestamps with tie-breaking)
This implementation does fan-out-on-write (pushing to each member's queue); explore when fan-out-on-read (pull model) is preferable, especially for large groups
See how watermark advance, late events, and dedup are tested; reveals edge cases the implementation handles
The design rationale and interview-framing that drove the implementation choices
This implementation uses tumbling windows; understand when you'd pick sliding or session windows instead and what changes structurally
How this single-node design would partition by `ad_id` across workers, and what the merge/reconciliation layer looks like
The watermark advance is the most complex method; trace through the finalization and pruning logic with overlapping windows in different states
See how the three strategies are tested and what edge cases the test suite covers (e.g., celebrity threshold crossover, pagination, unfollow cache invalidation)
The most complex read path; worth tracing how deduplication interacts with the push/pull split and whether post ordering is preserved correctly
The celebrity threshold is static: if a user crosses it mid-lifecycle, already-pushed posts stay in caches while new ones switch to pull. Worth exploring whether this causes feed gaps or duplicates
The relevance formula uses `log(1 + likes + 2*comments)` scaled by `RELEVANCE_WEIGHT=50.0`; explore how this balances recency vs. engagement and whether the weight makes older viral posts dominate
The external test suite likely covers edge cases beyond the inline `test_all()`, possibly concurrency simulation with threading
The two-phase OCC implementation is the architectural centerpiece; worth tracing how it would behave under real concurrency (e.g., with threading or async)
Compare this optimistic approach to pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE); understand when each is preferred in real hotel/booking systems
The gap between Phase 1 (read) and Phase 2 (verify) is zero in single-threaded code, but in a distributed system this is where overbooking bugs live — worth studying how databases close this gap
See how conflict detection, chunked uploads, and permission inheritance are exercised in tests
The version vector logic is the most interview-relevant part; trace through multi-device edit scenarios
Understand the design decisions and tradeoffs considered before implementation
Why version vectors were chosen over simpler conflict detection; how they scale with device count
Compare the object storage design: different chunking, deduplication, and metadata strategies for a related problem
How each component is exercised in isolation and integration; reveals the expected contracts and edge cases
Double hashing from a single MD5 digest; worth understanding why this approximates k independent hash functions
SimHash detects near-duplicate *documents*; MinHash detects near-duplicate *sets* — understanding when to use which is a common interview topic
The deferred-requeue mechanism for politeness; consider how this behaves under heavy load with many hosts vs. few hosts
The crawler uses the Bloom filter as a *gatekeeper* for the frontier; a false positive means a URL is never crawled — worth reasoning about acceptable FP rates in practice
The Merkle-tree-based all-pairs sync is the most sophisticated consistency mechanism in the codebase; worth understanding how it interacts with read repair

Three implementations (Payment, Message Queue, Ad Click) solve exactly-once differently (idempotency keys, consumer dedup, event-id registry); comparing their failure modes reveals when each approach breaks down
The three fan-out strategies are all implemented in this single method; trace how the hybrid path decides push vs. pull at the celebrity boundary
None of these implementations model network partitions, node crashes, or partial failures; exploring how each would behave under partition would reveal which consistency guarantees actually hold
The middleware and factory classes (lines 200–259) are prescribed in detail by the plan but weren't visible in the observations; verify per-path routing and header generation match the spec
Compare the plan's state machine (CREATED→PROCESSING→COMPLETED/FAILED) against the actual transitions in `process_payment` and `refund` to verify no unreachable states exist

All three systems use `current_time` injection for testability but handle the `None` default differently (rate limiter falls back to `time.time()`, chat system falls back to `0.0`); this inconsistency could cause subtle bugs in integration scenarios
This implementation uses tumbling windows; understand when you'd need sliding or session windows instead and how the data structures change
The `windows[ad_id]` keying is a natural partition key; explore how this maps to MapReduce or stream-processing topologies (e.g., Kafka Streams repartitioning by ad_id)
The most complex method; trace through the window lifecycle transitions and dedup pruning to understand the finalization guarantees
This implementation uses a single Lamport counter; explore how a distributed version would need vector clocks or hybrid logical clocks for multi-server causal ordering
The ring-ownership calculation uses a subtle modular arithmetic pattern worth studying for interview prep
How `num_virtual_nodes` affects load balance vs. memory, and why 150 is a common default (empirical sweet spot for 5-50 nodes)
The reserve-then-release pattern in `init_chunked_upload` / `complete_chunked_upload` has a subtle double-counting guard worth tracing carefully
See how the pipeline, counters, and recommendation engine are exercised; reveals edge cases the implementation handles
Trace how failure cascades work through the DAG; consider what happens when `thumbnail` fails but `transcode` succeeds
Compare Morris counter estimates against exact counts at various scales to understand the precision/memory tradeoff
The HLL implementation includes the linear counting correction for small cardinalities; worth understanding when it activates and why
Explore how reciprocal rank fusion blends strategies and how weight tuning affects feed composition
See how concurrency, daily limits, and transfer atomicity are verified in tests
Understand why transfers are excluded from the conservation check and what the 0.01 epsilon covers
Why production wallets use `Decimal` or integer-cents instead of `float`, and when the rounding strategy here breaks down
How this single-process locking model would change with multiple service instances (two-phase commit, saga pattern, event sourcing)
See how the service API is exercised end-to-end, especially threading and draft workflows
Linear scan search is the biggest scalability gap; worth exploring how a real system would use inverted indexes
The current implementation stores BCC addresses in the email dict visible to all recipients, which violates BCC semantics in a real system
Design decisions and trade-offs considered before implementation
Integer-offset cursors break under concurrent writes; worth comparing to token-based cursors used by Gmail API
Compare this round-robin assignment with Kafka's range/sticky assignors to understand the tradeoffs of this simplified approach
The `seen_message_ids` set grows unboundedly and lives in-memory — explore how Kafka solves this with idempotent producers and transactional offsets
See how the road network is constructed in tests and what edge cases are covered (disconnected graphs, one-way streets, alternative routes)
Yen's K-shortest paths has subtle correctness requirements around edge blocking and spur node selection; worth verifying against the reference algorithm
The time-optimized heuristic uses global max speed, which can be very loose on mixed-speed networks; explore how contraction hierarchies or ALT heuristics improve this
The design plan likely discusses system-level concerns (tile serving, caching, partitioning) that this implementation simplifies away
Alternative route algorithms: Yen's finds K-shortest, but real map apps use penalty-based or plateau methods to find *meaningfully different* routes, not just slightly different ones
The design rationale document; will explain what trade-offs were intentional vs. simplified for the exercise

The dynamic pricing reads current occupancy, but `reserve` computes price *after* the availability check and *before* the commit — consider whether this creates a TOCTOU window where the price could be stale
Whether storing multiple concurrent `VersionedValue` siblings constitutes a proper state-based CRDT, and what client-side merge semantics are assumed
How the alert state machine transitions are exercised, and edge cases around downsampling and retention
The alert state machine is the most complex logic; trace the PENDING→ALERTING transition timing and the RESOLVED→re-fire path
This system uses poll-based evaluation (caller invokes `evaluate_alerts`); compare with push-based evaluation triggered on ingest, and the tradeoffs for latency vs. computational cost
The sorted-list model has O(n) insertion; explore LSM-tree or columnar approaches (like Gorilla compression) used by real TSDB systems
The design rationale and requirements that shaped this implementation
Unit tests reveal the expected API usage patterns and edge cases the service was designed to handle
QA-level tests likely cover integration scenarios, boundary conditions around TTL and distance thresholds
Related geospatial system in the same repo; compare how it handles spatial indexing (likely geohash-based vs. grid-based here)
This implementation uses a uniform grid; geohashes (used by Redis GEO) handle the latitude-distortion problem and support variable precision — worth comparing approaches
The notification path doesn't use the grid index while the query path does; explore whether this is intentional or a missed optimization
The celebrity threshold is evaluated at write time, creating a potential inconsistency window when a user's follower count crosses the threshold between writes; worth reasoning about in an interview
The `_remove_author_from_cache` method does a full scan of the deque on unfollow; consider how this scales and what data structures (e.g., per-author index) could avoid O(n) scans
The `heapq.merge` approach assumes inputs are pre-sorted, but `get_user_posts` returns a freshly sorted list each call; consider how a persistent sorted index would change this
See how the deterministic time model and configurable failure rates are exercised in tests
The retry backoff logic and deferred-item re-enqueue pattern are the most complex control flow; trace through a multi-retry scenario
The `_pending_groups` dict and `_group_window` field are initialized but never used — this appears to be a planned notification batching/digest feature that was never implemented
Quiet hours use `datetime.UTC` exclusively; explore how a real system would handle per-user timezones
The state machine allows `REFUNDED` → `REFUND_PENDING` → `REFUNDED` for partial refunds, but there's no guard against concurrent refunds or a terminal `FULLY_REFUNDED` state
`get_balance()` does a full ledger scan on every call; in a real system this would need materialized views or running totals with reconciliation
How the two indexes are tested and whether edge cases (poles, antimeridian, coincident points) are covered
Related spatial problem (real-time nearby users) likely using different techniques — compare the index approach

The heart of write-path performance; trace through a multi-level insert to see how caches propagate bottom-up

Design decisions and tradeoffs documented before implementation
When predictability (counter) vs determinism (hash) matters in URL shortening — hash gives you deduplication for free, counter gives you guaranteed O(1) generation
Compare ID generation strategies — the unique ID generator likely tackles similar encoding/distribution problems in a distributed context
